sentences.text: I must be having a little cold.
Look at me.
We were in this brick factory for four weeks.
And during that time, we had to sleep in little slats-- were under a, under a, under long strips of-- they're not rooms, they're not houses, they were, they were used to dry out the bricks.
And so there were bricks for walls, not necessarily all the way to the, to the top, to the roof.
It just had roof, a roof, and it had beams here and there.
It had no natural walls, so it was totally open.

This was, this is important to explain because it was April-- April 15, 1944 when we were picked up and for-- during this month that-- for four weeks that we were in ghetto [NON-ENGLISH], the strong wind, a sharp, cold wind, swept through the ghetto, and the red brick dust was all over us.
It didn't take perhaps not even more than an hour before the red dust just, just penetrated every part of your body, even the area that was covered up.
It, it was incredible how the wind would sweep this red dust.
What would-- what was a very nice factory, as factories go, and in fact, a fairly new factory [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was not for the purpose that it, that it was being used.
The Germans occupied Hungary in, around, shortly before April 15th, and they decided to finish the job that the Hungarians did not do.

And so it happened that we were picked up, and we were in the outlying areas that Horthy wanted to, wanted the Hungarians to pick up first-- not the Hungarians in Budapest, but the outlying areas that became Hungary in '38.
And of course, when Horthy did not want to-- did not agree and did not want to pick up the Jews and send them to their deaths, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the country was overrun by the Germans.
Horthy Miklos.
He was the-- he was an admiral, and he was the ruler of Hungary.
And, and of course, he was replaced by a, a, a real anti-Semite.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then, of course, the Germans were in.

And so they did-- they finished the rest of his job, and after they finished picking up all the Jews in all the towns around [NON-ENGLISH], then we were shipped, shipped out in the middle of the night from ghetto [NON-ENGLISH].
It took us four days to-- before we reached our destination.

We realized that the-- this degrading process that, that was being used on us was not just a temporary situation, that once we were moved from ghetto [NON-ENGLISH], it-- we became suspicious that this, this may be lethal.
There were-- we were so crammed in, into cattle cars, that not all of us could, could sit at the same time.
Some of us had to doze off-- excuse me-- by standing up and by leaning on each other's shoulders.
With us was a barrel of water-- not a big barrel, but something like five or 10 gallons, possibly, and a bed pan.
Yes, we had to do our basic human functions with just a single blanket as a screen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Four days later, we arrived at our destination on a Friday evening, and all night long they kept the cattle cars locked.
And when daylight broke, my dad looked through the cracks of the cattle cars, and he commented that he did not like what he saw.
He noticed all the electric wire fences and rows and rows and rows of long barracks.
The name of the place, as we later learned, was Auschwitz-Birkenau.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the doors were shoved open and German voices screamed at us, [GERMAN], or out, out, and further orders were shouted at us to, to, to walk in a certain direction in an orderly fashion.
We arrived four days later.
So it was-- I don't want to be pinned down to the exact date, if you don't mind, because I know that our trip took four days.

So we arrived in, in Auschwitz, and men in striped uniform hopped up into the wagon, into the cattle cars, and bent over pretending to be doing this or that or picking up packages or luggage or bundles that we had along with us, and these men kept whispering to us, you are 16.
You are 14.
You are 15.
Tell them you can do, do work in the fields.
Tell them you were a farmer.
Tell them that you can-- you always worked hard.
This sort of thing.

And we were very confused anyway, and this truly added to our confusion, except that perhaps a little ray of hope, too, because it, it-- as I think about it, it must have made us feel that at least we will be able to work.
And that perhaps, if we tell them a certain-- give them a certain bit of information, perhaps they're going to put us in that type of work.
But we learned later what this really meant.
And children, all around us, seeing this cruelty, began to scream, and generally, we were all very scared.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This one Nazi officer in front had white gloves, a white, white gloves on his hands.
And in his right hand, he held a little stick or baton.
And he was very neatly dressed in such a dusty place in his fancy Nazi uniform, shiny black boots.
He used this little baton to separate us into three groups.
First, the men had to go one way, and the women had to go another way.

And then he sent all the-- approximately all those between the ages of 16 or 17 to approximately 50 into one group, and the children, the handicapped, the sick he sent in another way, and the men went through the same thing.
So we had these different directions to go to-- basically two directions, but the men and the women separately.
And so I was sent with my mother, and my sister, [NON-ENGLISH], who was 12 years old, was sent off with the children, the old and the young, the sick and the disabled.

And mother thought that [NON-ENGLISH] will be, will be well taken care of by the old people because the old people would take care of the young people, the children, maybe even cook for them, while we probably would have to work very hard, judging by the age group of our group.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but soon-- and so we started walking in the opposite direction.
And soon after [NON-ENGLISH] was separated from us, a wagon, pulled by two horses, was catching up from behind.
It carried the bundles and the luggage, whatever we brought along, from the trains and-- pulled by two horses, this wagon had rubber tires.
And as it began to pass us, we noticed that [NON-ENGLISH] was sitting in the back of it, looking for us in the rows as it pass, was passing us.
And she was sitting in the back with her feet dangling down as she did this.
And she, when she saw us, she just hopped off and joined us in the row, and she continued to walk with us in the direction that the wagon was going.

Nobody noticed her in the hoopla, in the big commotion, and-- but Mother was concerned and-- because she was afraid that [NON-ENGLISH] will not be able to keep up with us while we would have to work very hard.

But of course, we were also happy to have the family together, at least the three of us, so we continued to walk and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taken to a place where they, where they shaved our heads completely with a hand-operated hair-cutting machine, whatever they are called.
And in a minute, my hair was gone.
First, first, they actually snipped off my braids, and they just dumped it on that pile.
And then they took this, these-- I wish I knew the word for it-- hair-cutting device, and they just went through my head.
I couldn't believe that my, my hair was completely gone.

And as I touched it, I noticed a little blood on my hand because they did it so fast they cut into the scalp a little bit, which, nothing to complain about, but I thought I'd mention it because it, it was very crudely done.
When all of us were shaven, we looked at each other.
We looked more like scarecrows.
I could hardly recognize my mother anymore unless I looked straight into her blue eyes.
We really looked so different.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also, after this, received new clothes.
New-- I, I shouldn't have said new-- different clothes.
We had to give up all our clothing from home except for our shoes, which we were able to keep.
Luckily, I had good strong shoes with me which was made for Passover by a shoemaker who made it by hand, made to order.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then came the final dehumanization, the branding.
Henceforth, I no longer had a name.
I became a number, number A6374, tattooed on my left arm.
As you see, I'm still carrying that number on my arm today.
My mother was tattooed first, A6372, my sister followed with 73 and I with 74 so that we had consecutive tattoo numbers.

This was unusual only because, by this time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about an hour and a half later, there were hardly anybody left [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in whose families three people were still alive.
So to have three consecutive tattoo numbers became a very unusual [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] event-- well, thing to have.
Yes.
The instrument looked like a pen with an ink packet in it, probably.
It had a sharp point.
It was black, I remember, and two women held our arms down and then each time this, the needle was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put under the skin and withdrawn, the pen left a dot there.
And so it is that this number is made up of a whole series of dots left under the skin.
The following day, my arm swelled terribly from the tattooing, but after a while, the, the, the swelling had subsided.
Some people actually died from this process of tattooing because they, they used the same needle on everybody.
Well, they had a couple of people doing this, and so these couple of tattooing pens, if I may call it that, was used on hundreds and hundreds of women.
Well, the pain was sharp.
That's why they held our hands down, but it's much like getting a shot, and, and if, except that you've got quite a few shots.
I've never counted the number of dots, but that's besides the point, really.

This number indicates that I was, I was in, incarcerated in Auschwitz, and that I was-- actually, it was stamped for extermination, that-- but not before they would take full advantage of my strength.
This became so true [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because I don't think they expected us to live very long on the food that they gave us, which totaled-- it's very easy to actually add up the calories.
I would say approximately between 350 to 450 calories a day.
If I say 500 calories, I am already exaggerating.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we worked 12 hours a day.
We had to get up at, at the crack of dawn each morning and stand in line till 6:00 AM, at which time we would be counted.
And periodically, entire rows of us would be sliced off, sent away, and never to be seen again.
And sometimes this would be from one end of, of the row, or sometimes from the other end, so that we, we never knew where we would be safe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every morning, as we would march to work at 7:00 AM, we would always see human beings against the electric wire fences, like that.
These people had actually committed suicide.
All of us lost members from our families and most of us lost everyone, so naturally, we were quite sad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't really know, for a few days, where we were.
We had no idea that there was such a thing as a death camp.
We had no idea that human beings are exterminated like cattle-- not even that humanely-- on a mass basis.
I'll give you-- I'll draw a little picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as to how I came to this realization.
The first three days or so, we were in a special barracks after we arrived.

After the dehumanization process of shaving the head and tattooing us and receiving gray cotton uniforms, which were really much like rags, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were placed in this, these barracks where they were supposed to apparently make sure that we don't, didn't come with any contagious diseases.
In a death camp, mind you, that we didn't come with any, any contagious diseases and infect them, perhaps.
We just liked to sing, and how nice it would be to sing for the old timers.
And these were the Jews from Czechoslovakia, Germany, Austria, France, Poland, Germany, and so on.
And so we decided to, to pick 'Hatikvah.' We all knew the melody to 'Hatikvah.' And we didn't know the words in Hebrew yet to 'Hatikvah,' but we knew it-- we knew the words to it in Hungarian.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember knowing it in Czech, too, but I can't remember the words in Czech anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[DRINKS] [SINGING IN HUNGARIAN] We sang this song so many times that even the Polish inmates began to, to sing some of the words in Hungarian here and there, and they loved to hear it.
Of course, we sang other melodies, other songs from home that we all knew.
There was a little stool, and each of us would stand up on, on this little stool and sing to the old timers.
Yes.
I translated it in, in English, into English.
I know I spent a little time on it.

I did that in 1983 for, for San Francisco State University. 'There are many rows of barracks in a camp, somewhere in the world, by which rows and rows of Jews line up for Zahlappell with tear in their eyes.
Wretched, cold, and soaking wet, and the rain is pouring down, but for Zahlappell'-- that's head count-- 'they must line up regardless of the weather.
Be of good spirit and blossom, heroic Jewish worker.
Before too long will come the day of freedom.
The great day will come, the day of reckoning, when the Jewish suffering will end forever.
We will return to our beautiful homes, into the arms of long lost dear ones.
This is our song until we die, our Jewish heritage, we will never to die.' [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It is written by young girls-- composed, not written, but composed by young girls.
And for years, I, I wasn't very proud of it because it's not so polished.

And as I grew older, I kept looking and looking at the words, and I realized how hopeful it is and how naive we were, that here we were looking forward to returning home into the arms of our loved ones.
Little did we know that in this death camp, in this death factory, our loved ones went up in the smoke in the chimneys.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
